segments.s303-g49: همه دشت is a genitive/quantifying phrase meaning 'the whole plain' (همه before the noun = 'entire/whole'). The list مغز و سر و پای uses و to join coordinate nouns (brains, heads, feet). بود is a simple past copula ('was') agreeing with the singular/neuter collective subject 'دشت'.
segments.s303-g50: همانا is a rhetorical discourse marker 'verily/indeed'. مگر here functions idiomatically as an exception/limitive ('except/only' → giving the sense 'scarcely/except that'), followed by the locative phrase بر زمین جای بود ('there was place on the ground'); بود is past. Together they produce the sense 'there was hardly any place left on the earth.'
segments.s303-g51: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/constantly' placed before the verb phrase. نعلِ اسبان is a genitive construction 'the horses' horseshoes' (نعل + possessive noun). سرِ کشته is 'the head(s) of the slain' where کشته functions adjectivally/attributively. خست is an archaic/poetic past-form verbal predicate here (used statively) — translated roughly 'lay' or 'were (lying)/were trodden' in this context; it is rendered as past tense agreeing with the plural collective idea.
segments.s303-g52: همه دشت again = 'the whole plain' (an entire-area subject). بی‌تن is a compound adjective 'without body / disembodied' modifying دشت (poetic hyperbole meaning the plain was full of dismembered parts). The sequence سر و پای و دست lists body-parts in a coordinate series; the copula is omitted (common in Persian poetry), read as 'was (full of)'.
segments.s303-g53: خردمند مردم is an adjective + noun phrase 'the wise people' (in Persian the adjective may precede the noun). به یکسو شدند is an intransitive verbal phrase 'turned/moved to one side' where به یکسو is a directional adverbial and شدند is the past plural of شدن 'became/turned'. The whole means 'the wise people moved aside.'
segments.s303-g54: دو لشکر is 'two armies' (dual numeral + noun). بر این کار here means 'over/about/with respect to this affair' (بر + noun phrase indicating cause or subject matter). خستو شد(ند) — خستو is an adjective 'weary/exhausted' and شدند is the past plural copula, so 'the two armies became/grew weary.'
segments.s303-g55: که‌گر is a conditional conjunction 'if'. یک زمان نیز is an adverbial phrase 'even a single moment' (نیز adding emphasis 'even/also'). لشکر is the subject 'the army' and چنین is a predicate/adverbial 'thus/so' describing the state — word order is poetically flexible, placing چنین after the subject.
segments.s303-g56: بماند is the present-subjunctive/optative form of ماندن ('remain'), used in conditional clauses to express a hypothetical 'were to remain' or 'should remain'. بر این دشت is the locative 'on this plain' (بر + demonstrative + noun). با درد و کین ('with pain and hatred') is an instrumental/qualitative phrase describing the manner or consequence; درد here means 'suffering' and کین 'hatred/enmity.'
segments.s303-g97: This is a comparative noun phrase: شب تیره (noun + postpositive adjective) = 'the dark night', followed by a simile introduced by چون 'like/as'. روی زنگی سیاه is a genitive construction where روی 'face' is specified by زنگی 'a Zangi (person from Zanj)' and modified by the adjective سیاه 'black' (postpositive). The whole segment functions as a circumstantial simile describing the night.
segments.s303-g98: This is a simple past-clause: کس آمد 'someone came' (کس = indefinite subject, آمد = past tense 'came'), followed by a source phrase ز + اسم and a goal phrase به شاه 'to the king'. گستهم appears here as a name/place in apposition to نوذر (both preceding with the preposition ز marking origin), so the structure is 'someone came from [گستهم / نوذر] to the king.'
segments.s303-g99: که here introduces an exclamatory/optative sentence ('may ... live'). شاه جهان 'king of the world' is a title (noun + genitive), جاودان is a postpositive adjective 'eternal', and زنده‌باد is an optative verb phrase meaning 'may he live' or 'long live'. Word order is typical Persian: subject/title followed by the predicative optative.
segments.s303-g100: The second clause beginning with که gives the content or reason: که ما بازگشتیم 'that we returned' (ما = subject, بازگشتیم = past-tense plural 'we returned'), followed by predicates پیروز و شاد 'victorious and glad' as predicate adjectives. The coordinating و simply links the two adjectives.
segments.s303-g101: بدان here is an archaic/contracted locative/directional sequence (ب + آن = 'to those') introducing the indirect object/recipient; نامداران 'the renowned ones' modifies the following proper name افراسیاب (genitive/attributive). The phrase as a whole means 'to those renowned men of Afrasiab' and functions as the goal/target of رسیدیم in the next segment.
segments.s303-g102: رسیدیم is the past-tense plural verb 'we arrived/reached'; ناگه is an adverb 'suddenly' modifying the verb; به هنگام خواب is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time of sleep' (i.e., while [he] slept). Word order places the verb before the temporal phrase, a common poetic option.
segments.s303-g103: از ایشان 'from/among them' is a prepositional phrase indicating source or group, followed by the noun phrase سواری طلایه (rider/horseman + طلایه 'vanguard/forefront'). نبود is the past negative 'was not' (there was not). The clause states absence of a vanguard rider in their ranks.
segments.s303-g104: This clause is existential/negational: کسی را (indefinite 'anyone' with the object/ topical marker را used for emphasis) + ز اندیشه مایه نبود 'there was no mây-eh (resource/seed/cause) of thought' — idiomatically 'no one had any thought/plan/initiative'. The negation is on the verb نبود 'was not'.
segments.s303-g185: This is a compact locative + verb clause in classical Persian: به تاریکی (preposition به + noun) gives an adverbial 'into/in the darkness'; اندر طلایه places the thing seen ('in the vanguard') with اندر = 'in/within'; بدید is the past 3rd‑person singular of دیدن ('he saw'). Word order is topical: location first, then the thing (طلایه) in an اندر‑phrase, then the past verb بدید.
segments.s303-g186: Parallel clause to the first: به شهر اندر is another locative ('in/into the town'); آواز ایشان is a genitival/nominal phrase 'their voice/cries' (آواز + ایشان = 'their cry'); شنید is the past 3rd singular of شنیدن ('he heard'). The verb is again final, and ایشان refers back to the previously mentioned people.
segments.s303-g187: فروماند is a past participial/predicative verb meaning 'was cast down/worn out'. ز آن کار gives the cause ('by/from that affair'); رستم is the (postposed) subject; شگفت is an adjectival comment 'astonished' placed after the name as a brief epithet. Classical Persian often puts cause or circumstance (ز آن کار) before the subject+predicate.
segments.s303-g188: همی راند uses همی to mark continuous or iterative past action ('kept on, was riding/driving') and راند is the past 3rd‑person of راندن ('he rode/caused to ride'). The conjunction و links the next clause; اندیشه اندر گرفت is a causative/inchoative idiom where گرفت (past of گرفتن) takes اندیشه as its internal object and means 'thought seized him' i.e. 'he was seized by thought/worry' — here گرفت behaves as 'be seized' (intransitive reading with emotional subject).
segments.s303-g189: همه کوفته لشکر is a noun phrase with an attributive past participle: همه 'the whole' + کوفته (participial adjective 'struck/overthrown') + لشکر 'army' — literally 'the whole, struck army'. The following و ریخته continues the participial description: ریخته is another past participle ('scattered/fallen'), so together they describe the army's state. Classical Persian often chains participles like this without an explicit auxiliary.
segments.s303-g190: به شیرین روان اندر آویخته is an adjunct expressing direction/attachment: به + name/epithet (شیرین روان = 'Shirin‑ravān' or 'sweet‑souled Shirin') marks the target, اندر آویخته is a compound participial phrase meaning 'hung on / clung to / attached to'. The participle آویخته (past participle of آویختن) describes the state resulting from the preceding scattering/defeat.
segments.s303-g191: This is a locative nominal clause: به پیش اندرون ('at the fore/front inside') sets the place, then رستم تیزچنگ is the named occupant (Rostam Tizchang). Classical Persian poetry often omits the copula 'was' and simply juxtaposes place and person: place first, then the person present there.
segments.s303-g192: پس پشت here functions as a compound locative 'at the rear/behind'; شاه و سواران جنگ is a coordinated noun phrase ('the king and the war‑horsemen' / 'riders of battle'). Again the verb 'were' is omitted by poetic style — the two halves contrast front (previous segment) and rear (this segment).
segments.s303-g105: چو ... گشتند is a temporal clause: چو here = 'when' and گشتند is the past plural of گشتن ('became/turned/awoke'), so the verb precedes its subject for poetic word order. ز ایشان سران = 'the chiefs/heads among them' (ز used partitive/agentive), so the whole segment means 'when the chiefs of them awoke.'
segments.s303-g106: کشیدیم is past first-person plural 'we drew' (here transitive, with the sense 'draw (a weapon)'). The direct objects شمشیر و گرز گران follow it: 'sword and heavy mace,' with گران a postpositive adjective modifying گرز (and implicitly شمشیر as a coordinated object).
segments.s303-g107: چو شب روز شد is a temporal formula 'when night became day' (i.e. at dawn). جز قراخان نماند is an inverted clause meaning 'none remained except Qara Khan' — جز = 'except' and نماند is past negative/preterite sense 'was left / remained (none)'.
segments.s303-g108: ز marks partitive/agentive 'of/from their men': ز مردان ایشان فراوان نماند = 'not many of their men remained.' فراوان here qualifies the quantity and nماند negates 'remained' — overall a negative statement about remaining numbers.
segments.s303-g109: همه دشت 'the whole plain' is the topic; ز ایشان gives cause/possession 'because of them / of them.' سرون و سر is a poetic pairing (سرون = 'cypresses' i.e. tall poles or spears; سر = 'heads' here, literal heads or prominent tops), and است is the copula asserting that the plain consisted of those elements. Word order is poetic but functions as 'the whole plain was cypresses and heads of them.'
segments.s303-g110: This is an equative clause with است as copula: 'زمین بستر و خاکشان چادر است' = 'the ground is (their) bed and their dust is a tent.' خاکشان is a possessive with -شان 'their.' The two nominal phrases are linked by و and presented as metaphoric landscape imagery.
segments.s303-g111: به مژده = 'with/bringing (good) news' (به used prepositionally), ز رستم = 'from Rostam.' هم اندر زمان groups as 'also/at the same time' (هم + اندر زمان) indicating simultaneity. Overall: 'with news from Rostam, also at that moment...' (prepares for the following verb).
segments.s303-g112: بیامد is past 'came.' هیونی is an animate/ferocious creature (here 'a savage beast/monster'), and سپیده‌دمان is an adverbial time 'at daybreak.' The clause states the arrival of that creature at dawn.
segments.s303-g17: چو آمد is a short temporal clause: چو (here = “when”) precedes the past verb آمد (“came”) to mark a single past event. به نزدیک + noun is a prepositional phrase meaning “near/close to X” (به + نزدیک + place), so the whole segment reads “when (they) came near Gulzarion.”
segments.s303-g18: زمین شد is a simple past/resultative: زمین (ground/earth) + شد (became). به سانِ که is a fixed comparative phrase meaning “in the manner that / such that,” followed by a nominative place name (بیستون) as the point of comparison. Literally: “the ground became in the manner that Bisotun (is).”
segments.s303-g19: همی before the verb gives a continuous or iterative sense (“kept / was still”), so همی لشکر آمد reads “the army kept coming.” The time phrase سه روز و سه شب follows the verb and expresses the duration (“for three days and three nights”).
segments.s303-g20: جهان شد is again subject + شد (‘the world became’). پرآشوب is a predicative adjective placed before the noun it describes in poetry (“full of turmoil/tumultuous”), and جنگ و جلب list the kinds of turmoil (literally “war and jalb”), giving the sense “the world became full of the turmoil of war and jalb.”
segments.s303-g21: کشیدند is a past plural verb “they drew/ stretched”; here it takes an object phrase بر هفت فرسنگ نخ, where بر functions as ‘on / over’ and هفت فرسنگ specifies the length/extent (“seven parasangs”), نخ (‘thread/line’) naming what was drawn—i.e. “they drew a line over seven parasangs.”
segments.s303-g22: فزون گشت is an adjectival phrase + past verb meaning ‘became abundant / increased’; مردم is the affected noun (“people”). ز here functions as the comparative/ablative “than/from,” so ز مور و ملخ yields the comparison “more numerous than ants and locusts.” Word order (adjective-verb-noun) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s303-g23: چهارم is an ordinal adjective (“fourth”) modifying سپه (“host/army”). برکشیدند is a past plural verb “they raised/drew up,” and صف is the direct object (“line/formation”), so the clause means “the fourth host drew up its ranks.”
segments.s303-g24: ز دریا برآمد is a simple past clause with ز دریا (“from the sea”) as source and برآمد “arose/issued forth.” به خورشید is a directional prepositional phrase (“toward the sun”), and تف (literally “spit”) is used image‑wise as the thing that rose — “a spitting (spray/jet) toward the sun.” The line has an abrupt, pictorial poetic order typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s303-g89: برافروختند is a past third‑person plural verb (‘they kindled’); the verb appears before its object (آتش) as a common poetic inversion. از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘from every direction/side’ modifying how/where the fires were kindled; سوی here means ‘side/direction’.
segments.s303-g90: طلایه is the subject ‘vanguard/forefront’ and بیامد is the past tense verb ‘came’ (here narrational past). ز is the poetic variant of از ‘from’, and هر پهلوی means ‘from every flank/side’ (پهلوی = flank/side). The clause reads straightforwardly ‘the vanguard came from every flank.’
segments.s303-g91: همی before the verb gives an iterative/continuous sense (‘kept on / was’) so همی ... ساخت reads ‘was making/raised’ in narrative imperfective. جنگ را marks ‘the war’ as the definite object (را), and افراسیاب at the end is the topical/subject position common in epic diction: ‘Afrasiab was making the war.’
segments.s303-g92: همی بود is again a continuous past sense (‘it continued/was’). تا is temporal ‘until’, introducing the limit ‘چشمه آفتاب’ literally ‘the spring of the sun’ (a poetic image, here indicating a time or source). بود is past copula. The clause means ‘it continued until the spring of the sun.’
segments.s303-g93: بر آید is a compound verb (بر + آید) functioning as present/historical present ‘rises/appears’. رخ کوه (‘the face of the mountain’) is the subject, and رخشان کند uses کند with رخشان (radiant) to mean ‘(it) makes/turns (it) radiant’ or simply ‘shines’. The verbs are narrative-present/poetic, coordinating to describe appearance and radiance.
segments.s303-g94: زمین is the entity affected (‘the earth’). چون introduces a simile ‘like/as’: چون نگین بدخشان = ‘like a gem of Badakhshan.’ بدخشان is adjectival/genitive ‘of Badakhshan’. کند again is the verb (‘makes/turns into’), so the clause says the earth was made like a Badakhshan jewel.
segments.s303-g95: جهان‌آفرین (‘world‑creator’, i.e. God) is fronted and followed by را used as a topic/emphasis marker here (not a simple object marker). دگر بود رای literally ‘there was a different counsel/plan’ — دگر = ‘other/different’, بود = past ‘was’, رای = ‘counsel/plan.’ The phrase states that the Creator’s plan was different.
segments.s303-g96: به هر کار = ‘in/for every affair’. با رای او = ‘with his counsel’ (prepositional phrase modifying the following predicate). نیست پای is an idiom ‘there is no end/limit’; نیست = ‘is not/does not exist’ and پای = ‘end/limit’ here. The full clause: ‘in every matter, with his counsel there is no limit.’
segments.s303-g169: 'به رستم' is an indirect-object prepositional phrase meaning 'to Rostam' placed before the verb; 'بگفت' is the past tense (classical/poetic) verb 'he said/told' (the prefix ب‑ is an older narrative form). 'آنچه پیغام بود' is a nominal clause 'what the message was' with 'بود' as the past copula; that entire clause functions as the object of بگفت.
segments.s303-g170: 'که' here introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/since/that'); 'فرجام پیغامش' is a genitive-like grouping where 'فرجام' (outcome/end) is linked to 'پیغامش' (the message + enclitic -ش 'its/his/their'), and 'آرام بود' is the predicate 'was peaceful/calm' with 'بود' as past copula.
segments.s303-g171: 'وز این روی' is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic locution meaning 'for this reason/therefore' (here 'وز' is an archaic/poetic variant related to 'از'). 'کیخسرو' is the proper name and 'کینه‌جوی' is a postpositive adjective/epithet 'vengeful, seeking revenge' describing him.
segments.s303-g172: 'نشسته' is the past participle used predicatively here meaning 'sat' or 'was seated'; 'به آرام' is an adverbial phrase 'in/with calmness' formed with 'به' + noun; 'بی‌گفت و گوی' is a negative adverbial 'without words/without speaking' (بی‑ = 'without', گوی here = 'speech/saying' in a literary register).
segments.s303-g173: 'همی کرد' is a frequentative/classical construction meaning 'kept doing' or 'continually did'; 'بخشش همه' is the object 'gifts/grants (all)' (بخشش here = donations/gifts); 'بر سپاه' is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the army' with بر marking the recipient/target.
segments.s303-g174: This is a simple enumerative list of the objects given: 'سراپرده' (inner chamber/curtain), 'خیمه' (tent), 'تاج' (crown), joined by و 'and'. 'گاه' ends the list; in this position it is a poetic word whose specific sense (here) is less literal — it rounds off the list ('and such/occasionally/place' depending on context).
segments.s303-g175: 'از ایرانیان' is a prepositional phrase 'from among the Iranians'; 'کشتگان' is the definite plural noun 'the slain' (those killed); the object marker 'را' marks کشتگان as definite object, and 'بجست' is the past tense 'sought/searched for' (classical form).
segments.s303-g176: 'کفن کرد' = 'he shrouded (them)' (کفن = shroud, کرد = did/made); 'وز' here links clauses in a classical/poetic way (roughly 'and then' or 'and'); 'خون و گلشان بشست' = 'he washed their blood and mud/dirt' with -شان 'their' on گل and بشست the past-tense verb 'washed'.
segments.s303-g9: جز از (جز + از) introduces an exception: 'except from'. چیزی نبینیم is negative with the 1pl ending -یم; here نبینیم functions as a simple past/epic present meaning 'we see / we do not see anything.' راه at the end is the noun 'way' (method/path) placed after the verb for emphasis: 'we see no way except war.'
segments.s303-g10: زبونی is a noun 'cowardice/abase­ment' used as the topic/predicate; نه خوبست is the negated copula (نه + خوبست = 'is not good'). چندین سپاه is a postposed quantified noun phrase 'many an army / several hosts' that specifies who should not show زبونی; Persian often places such modifiers after the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s303-g11: بگفتند is past tense 'they said.' وز here is a compressed poetic sequence (و + ز) meaning 'and from' or 'and then' (the listed senses do not include this idiomatic use, so choose -1 if none match); پیش 'before/in front' indicates temporal or spatial relation. برخاستند is past 'they rose / stood up.' The whole clause links as 'they said, and then rose up (from there).'
segments.s303-g12: همه شب = 'all night' (temporal phrase). همی is an archaic/epic particle marking continuous or habitual action ('kept on / were continuously'). لشکر is the direct object 'army/host' and آراستند is past 'they adorned / they arrayed' — together meaning 'they spent the whole night arraying the host.'
segments.s303-g13: سپیده دمان گاه is a temporal phrase 'at the break of dawn' (سپیده = dawn; دمان = edge/hem/limit of the dawn; گاه = time). بانگ خروس is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the cry of the cock' placed after the temporal phrase to indicate the sound occurring at that time.
segments.s303-g14: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' درگاه is the source/place 'threshold / gate / court.' برخاست is past 'rose (up) / arose.' آوای + کوس is a possessive/attributive pair 'the sound/voice of the drum' (کوس = war-drum), so the clause reads 'from the gateway arose the sound of the drum.'
segments.s303-g15: سپاهی functions as a collective noun 'a host / an army.' به هامون بیامد = 'came to the plain' (به + place; بیامد past 'came'). ز گنگ here links an accompanying manner phrase: ز + adjective 'گنگ' = 'with a muffled/indistinct sound' (the preposition ز introducing the circumstance), so 'a host came to the plain with a muffled sound.'
segments.s303-g16: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that / so ... that.' بر مور و بر پشه is a figurative expression 'on/for the ant and for the gnat' (i.e., even the smallest creatures); شد is past 'became'; راه تنگ is the predicate 'the way became narrow.' Together: 'so that the way became narrow even for ants and gnats' (i.e., the road was so crowded/narrow).
segments.s303-g25: به + قلب اندر is a compact, poetic directional phrase: به (toward/into) + قلب (heart) + اندر (in/within) = 'into the very heart/center'. افراسیاب و ردان are two coordinated nouns in apposition (Afrasiab and the Radan); و simply links them.
segments.s303-g26: سواران is the head noun 'horsemen/riders' and the two following words are postpositive adjectives: گردنکش (literally 'neck-throwing') = 'headstrong/rebellious', و links to بخردان which is a plural adjective/noun form meaning 'the wise/prudent'. In Persian epic style adjectives often follow the noun and are coordinated.
segments.s303-g27: سوی (toward/on the side of) + میمنه (the right side) marks a direction or flank. جهن افراسیاب is a name phrase in apposition (Jahan, Afrasiab) identifying who is on that flank; the proper names follow the directional phrase in typical poetic order.
segments.s303-g28: همی is the archaic/epic progressive particle indicating ongoing or repeated action here; نیزه is the direct object 'spear'; بگذاشت is the simple past classical form ('placed/raised' with the verbal prefix ب- attached) meaning 'he set/raised'; از آفتاب is a source- or similative phrase 'from the sun / of the sun' used poetically to mean 'gleaming like the sun' (آفتاب here = radiance).
segments.s303-g29: وز این روی is an idiomatic/archaic expression meaning 'for this reason' (وز here is an older form related to از + این). کیخسرو is the subject; از + قلبگاه ('from the heart/center') is a prepositional phrase specifying origin or position ('from the core/center of himself').
segments.s303-g30: همی داشت is the continuous/epic past construction ('was holding/kept'); چون introduces a simile 'like/as'; کوه پشت سپاه is the simile target: کوه 'mountain' qualifying پشت سپاه 'the army's rear' (پشت = rear/behind, followed by its possessor سپاه). The whole means he kept the army's rear as firm as a mountain.
segments.s303-g31: چو is the simile marker 'like/as' repeated for two parallels: گودرز (Goudarz) and طوس (Tous). نوذر نژاد is a postpositive compound meaning 'of Nowzar's lineage' or 'Nowzar‑born' (نوذر = the king Nowzar, نژاد = lineage/descended from), modifying طوس (Tous) or the following names by lineage reference.
segments.s303-g32: منوشان is a personal name (Manushan); خوزان is a geographic/ethnic tag 'of Khuzan' (Khuzan/Khuz people) placed after the name in classical style; پیروز و داد are coordinated epithets 'victorious and just' (داد here used as 'justice/just').
segments.s303-g1: This is a prepositional/locative phrase giving origin/location. وز (here an archaic or poetic preposition 'from') begins the phrase, then آن سوی گنگ = 'that side (of the Ganga)'; اندر is the classical preposition 'in/within', and افراسیاب is the place-holder/name. Word order is typical older Persian: preposition + demonstrative + noun + 'in' + proper name.
segments.s303-g2: Two temporal adverbials joined by و. به رخشنده روز = 'by/at the radiant day' and به هنگام خواب = 'and at the time of sleep'; the preposition به here makes the noun phrase adverbial (English 'by'/'at'). رخشنده modifies روز; هنگام + noun is a fixed pattern meaning 'at the time of X'.
segments.s303-g3: همی گفت is an iterative/imperfective phrasing (همی + past form) giving habitual or continuous speech — 'he kept saying' or 'he would say'. با + هرکه = 'to/with whoever'; بد کاردان is a compound adjective meaning 'badly skilled / ill-advised' describing the addressee. Note that classical word order places the verb before the indirect object phrase.
segments.s303-g4: This is the content of what he says: بزرگان بیدار و بسیاردان — 'the great ones are awake and very learned'. Adjectives follow the nouns they modify; و simply coordinates the two adjectival predicates.
segments.s303-g5: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که 'that/since': که اکنون که ... رسید = 'for now that ... has arrived'. The repeated که is emphatic/subordinating (classical style). به بالین رسید is a verbal phrase meaning 'reached the bedside' (بالین = pillow/bedside; رسید = past 'arrived').
segments.s303-g6: به گنگ اندرون = 'into the muffled/quiet inner room' (به + noun gives direction/place). چون توان آرمید is a rhetorical question: چون = 'how', توان (modal) = 'could', and آرمید is past 'rested/slept' — together 'how could he sleep?'. The clause thus expresses impossibility given the enemy's approach.
segments.s303-g7: همه برگشادند گویا زبان is an idiomatic description: همه برگشادند = 'everyone opened (their mouths / spoke)', گویا = 'as if/it seemed', and زبان here means 'tongue/speech' (i.e. 'as if with one tongue'). The verb برگشادند is past plural, matching همه.
segments.s303-g8: The clause introduced by که repeats the reason/observation: اکنون که نزدیک شد = 'now that he had drawn near', شد is simple past 'became' / auxiliary of occurrence, and بد گمان is a fixed adjective phrase meaning 'of ill-suspicion / filled with misgiving'. Word order places the verbal predicate after the adverbial/subject elements in classical style.
segments.s303-g249: به دیوار is a prepositional phrase giving location ('to/at the wall'); عراده is the thing being placed (military wagon) and بر پای is a locative idiom 'at the foot/base of'. کرد is the simple past transitive verb 'he placed/caused'. Word order is topical location + object + local phrase + verb, a normal classical Persian order.
segments.s303-g250: به برج اندرون is another locative phrase ('into/to the inner part of the tower'); رزم را uses را as the definite/object marker marking رزم ('the fighting/force') as object; جای کرد is a compound meaning 'placed/stationed'. The verb خاتمه (کرد) completes the clause and shows who did the placing.
segments.s303-g251: بفرمود is the honorific/past-tense verb 'he ordered/commanded'. تا here introduces the subordinate clause 'that/so that' and links it to the command. سنگهای گران is the object phrase 'heavy stones' (سنگهای plural + adjective گران).
segments.s303-g252: کشیدند is past plural 'they drew/hailed/pulled' (the agents carrying out the order). بر باره is a prepositional phrase 'onto/ upon the burden/load' and افسونگران is placed after it as the possessor/descriptor 'of the enchanters' (i.e., the enchanters' load). Together they form the complement of کشیدند.
segments.s303-g253: بخواند is past tense 'he called/summoned'. کاردانان رومی is the noun phrase 'skilled/able Romans' that is the object of بخواند; بسی here functions as an adverb 'very/many' (classical usage) modifying the noun phrase. Word order places the modifier before the noun phrase followed by the verb.
segments.s303-g254: سپاهی is 'soldiers/force' (the thing being set); به دیوار دژ is a locative phrase 'on the wall of the fortress' (به = to/onto, دیوار = wall, دژ = fortress). برنشاند is a causative/perfective verb meaning 'placed/caused to be set', completing the clause.
segments.s303-g255: برآورد is a past-tense verb here meaning 'brought forth/caused to be brought' — a classical, somewhat polyvalent verb; it is followed by an epithet + title: بیدار دل 'wakeful-hearted' (adjective + noun) qualifying جاثلیق (the Catholicos), which is the entity acted on or presented by the verb depending on context.
segments.s303-g256: بر آن باره is a locative/prepositional phrase 'upon that burden/instance' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, باره = burden/load). عراده و منجنیق are coordinated items 'wagons and mangonels' placed in apposition as what is set upon that burden; the whole phrase completes or specifies what برآورد did.
segments.s303-g201: A simple noun list joined by و (and). The sequence names possessions or people: زن (woman/wife) + کودک (child) + گنج (treasure). چِندان (چندان) here is a quantifier modifying سپاه and means “so many / such a large” and directly precedes the head noun سپاه (army). Word order is: nouns separated by و, with the quantifier immediately before the noun it limits.
segments.s303-g202: Another coordinated noun phrase: بزرگی (greatness, grandeur) و فرمان (command/authority) و تخت (throne) و کلاه (hat/helmet). There is no copula; the nouns form a paratactic list (typical in epic), so the predicate link to the previous list is implied rather than explicitly stated.
segments.s303-g203: بر + آن باره + دژ is a locative/locational phrase: بر (on/upon) governs the following phrase. آن باره is a demonstrative phrase ('that bāreh') modifying دژ (fortress); poetic word order places the modifier and demonstrative before the head noun. نپرد is the negative present form of پریدن (پَرِد / نپرد = ‘does not fly’), and عقاب (eagle) follows the verb as subject — a Verb–Subject order common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s303-g204: نبیند is the negative present (ن + بین‌stem) meaning 'does not see' and here functions as a general negative (no one sees). کسی (kasi) with that negative yields 'no one'. آن بلندی ('that height' / 'that loftiness') is the thing not seen; به خواب ('in a dream') indicates the sphere (i.e., even in sleep/dream). Word order is Verb–Subject–Object/adjunct (poetic inversion); the copula is absent because نبیند is a lexical verb.
segments.s303-g205: A nominal clause with existential/copular هست after the first noun: خورش هست = 'there is food' (خورش = cooked food/stew). The subsequent nouns (ایوان, گنج, سپاه) are added by و (and) in a simple coordinate list; هست functions as the copula/existential for the whole list rather than being repeated.
segments.s303-g206: ترا is the archaic/dialectal clitic form of تو را ('to/for you') used as a dative/topic marker. رنج بدخواه را ('the ill‑wisher’s suffering') carries the object/topic marker را (emphatic/definite). In poetic Persian a copula is often omitted; تاج و گاه ('crown and occasion' / ‘crown and time’) functions as the predicate: literally 'to you, the ill-wisher's suffering (is) crown and occasion.' The construction uses topicalization (ترا) and an object-marked noun as the semantic subject of a nominal predicate with an omitted است.
segments.s303-g207: همان بوم ('that very land') is followed by the relative/locative کو (here acting like 'which'/'where'), then را (topic/definite marker). بهشت است نام literally 'is named Paradise' — نام (name) follows the copula است in a poetic order: 'is name Paradise' → 'is called Paradise.' The relative phrasing and the postposed نام reflect elevated/archaic word order.
segments.s303-g208: همه جای = 'every place' or 'everywhere' (همه modifying جای). شادی و آرام و کام is a coordinated list of abstract nouns (joy, calm/peace, and fulfillment/desire). No copula is necessary; the phrase describes the quality of that land: 'everywhere [is] joy, peace and fulfillment.'
segments.s303-g225: به ایوان فرود آمد is a simple past clause: فرود آمد (past of فرود آمدن) = 'descended / came down', with the goal introduced by به ایوان ('to/into the portico'). The following و بار داد is a conjoined past clause: بار (object) precedes داد (past of دادن) without را, so بار داد here means 'gave/delivered the load' or 'unloaded/distributed goods.' Word order (object before verb) and the omitted object marker are typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s303-g226: سپه را is a definite/deflected NP marked by را (here marking the (definite) recipient/target). درم داد and و دینار داد are two coordinated small clauses with داد (past of دادن) as the verb: 'gave dirhams' and 'gave dinars' to the marked recipient. The verb is repeated for emphasis/clarity in each coordinated part.
segments.s303-g227: فرستاد is past third-person of فرستادن 'to send/dispatch'. بر هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'toward every side/direction' and لشکری is the sent material ('troops'/'a military detachment') completing the verb. Classical word order places the prepositional phrase before the object here.
segments.s303-g228: This phrase continues the object specification: نگهبان 'guardian/guard' is what was sent, هر لشکری 'for each army' qualifies who they are for, and مهتری is a postpositive descriptor meaning 'a chief/elder' — i.e., 'a chief guardian for each army.' The whole fragment is elliptically governed by the earlier فرستاد.
segments.s303-g229: پیاده here denotes 'infantry/foot soldiers' (a noun) and is the element being placed; بر آن باره is a locative/occasional phrase 'on/for that occasion/task' (باره = 'occasion, burden, task'), and بر دیده‌بان indicates the specific location 'on the lookout/posts.' The repeated بر marks spatial/functional relations in series; the clause is elliptical and ties back to the sending/placement verb from the previous line.
segments.s303-g230: نگهبان begins the predicate idea 'guards', then به‌روز 'by day' and به شب 'by night' give temporal adverbials (به‌روز literally 'of the day' used as 'by day'); و links them and پاسبان (another word for guard) specifies the night duty. The construction states roles by time of day: 'guard in daytime and watchman at night.'
segments.s303-g231: رد و موبدش is a compound subject: رد (here likely an item such as a robe or garment — an older/poetic noun) with موبدش 'his mobed' (موبد + possessive -ش). بود is the past copula 'was', and بر دست راست is a locative phrase 'at/on the right hand' (دست راست = 'right hand/side'). The line states the placement/position of those persons/objects.
segments.s303-g232: نویسنده نامه را is a definite object (نامه modified writer, marked by را). پیش خواست is a compound use where پیش (in the presence of / before) plus خواست (past of خواستن) yields 'he caused X to come forward / summoned X.' The whole phrase means 'he summoned the writer of the letter (into his presence).' Note the classical compound verb and the object-marker را.
segments.s303-g233: یکی نامه is an indefinite noun phrase: یکی 'one / a certain' before نامه 'letter' (object). نزدیک + فغفور چین functions as an attributive/prepositional phrase specifying the addressee: in classical Persian construction this reads like 'نزدِ فغفورِ چین' — 'at/unto the Faghfur of China' (the prepositional relation is implicit/elliptical). فغفور چین is a title (Faghfur = Caesar/Emperor of China).
segments.s303-g234: نبشتند is an archaic/perspective past plural form (classical spelling corresponding to نوشتند) meaning 'they wrote'. با introduces an instrumental/associative phrase 'with...', here quantifying the honorific formula. صد هزار آفرین is a grandeur formula 'a hundred thousand afarin(s)' where آفرین functions as 'praises/acclamations'.
segments.s303-g235: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین before گفت is a sentential adverb modifying the speech). کز is the fused classical particle (= که + از) meaning 'from/of/through', so کز گردش روزگار = 'from the turning/circuit of fate' and provides the causal/locative source for what follows.
segments.s303-g236: نیامد is past tense 'did not come / did not befall'. مرا here is the indirect/direct object ('to me' / 'for me') but tokenization split it into م + را: م is an orthographic fragment here (use -1 for sense), را is the definite/object marker attaching to the following noun بهره 'share/portion'. جز introduces the exception 'except', so the phrase reads 'a share did not come to me except (for) battle' (کارزار = 'battle').
segments.s303-g237: بپروردم is past first-person 'I reared/fostered' (prefix ب- marks the simple past in classical style). آن را که introduces a relative clause 'that which...', where که + بایست + کشت yields 'that which must be killed/that ought to be slain' — بایست is a modal 'ought / must' and کشت here is the substantive/act 'killing' (i.e. 'be killed').
segments.s303-g238: کنون شد is a temporal + copular sequence 'now has become'. از او marks cause/reason 'because of him / on his account'. روزگارم is the noun 'my fate/fortune' (possessive suffix -م) and درشت is a predicative adjective 'rough/harsh', so the clause means 'now, because of him, my fortune has grown harsh.'
segments.s303-g239: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when / if'. فغفور چین‌گر is a compound title naming the emperor (classical epithets are often compounded); چین‌گر here is an agentive/title element attached to فغفور. بیاید is a present/subjunctive form 'comes / may come' and is followed by رواست, a fixed predicative 'it is fitting / proper', so the clause reads 'When the Faghfur-of-China comes, it is fitting...'.
segments.s303-g240: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause. بر مهر او = 'because of / by his favour/affection' (بر + noun + pronoun). بر روانم = 'upon my soul' (again بر + noun with pronominal suffix). گواست is an archaic/polite predicative meaning 'is witness / attests' (here treated as the main verb of the subordinate clause), so the clause says roughly 'that, by his favour, he stands witness upon my soul.'
segments.s303-g273: پری چهره is a compound adjective‑noun meaning 'fairy‑faced' (literally 'fairy face') placed before the counted phrase; هر روز is an adverbial of frequency 'every day', and صد چنگ‌زن is the counted noun phrase 'a hundred harpists' (number + occupation follows the time expression). The whole segment supplies the subject/agent of the following verb.
segments.s303-g274: شدندی is an archaic past plural form of شدن ('they became' / 'they were made') common in epic Persian; it carries the main past action 'gathered/formed'. به درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase ('toward/into the king's court' — here locative 'in/at the royal presence'), and انجمن (assembly) names what was formed there. The verb therefore links the subject in the previous segment to this location and result.
segments.s303-g275: شب و روز ('night and day') is a time phrase meaning 'constantly'; چون here means 'when/as'. مجلس آراستی is 'you adorned/arranged the assembly' — آراستی is the classical past (2nd person singular) of آراستن, so this clause gives the temporal/background action ('as you set the court').
segments.s303-g276: سرود is a noun 'song'; از لب ترک is a source phrase 'from the Turk's lip(s)'. و می‌خواستی is coordinated to mean 'and you desired/asked for it' — می‌خواستی is the imperfect/habitual second‑person form ('you would want' / 'you desired') commonly used in narrative to express repeated or customary action. Word order places the desired object ('song from the Turk') before the verb that expresses desire.
segments.s303-g277: همی داد is a habitual past construction ('he kept giving' / 'he would give'); همی adds continuous or habitual aspect to the past verb داد ('gave'). هر روز گنجی به باد is 'a treasure each day to the wind' — به باد ('to the wind') is an idiom meaning to squander or give away heedlessly. The clause as a whole says he habitually wasted treasures daily.
segments.s303-g278: بر امروز و فردا is a prepositional phrase 'regarding/about today and tomorrow' (بر + time nouns), نیامدش is past negative 'did not come to him' where the enclitic -ش = 'to him', and یاد is the noun 'memory/thought'. Together the clause means 'the thought of today or tomorrow did not occur to him' — i.e. he gave no thought to present or future consequences.
segments.s303-g265: سپه را: 'سپه' (army) is marked by the direct-object/definiteness marker 'را', so this phrase names the recipient/affected group. درم داد: 'درم' (coin/money) is the object and 'داد' is the simple past 'gave'. و آباد کرد: the conjunction 'و' links a second verb phrase; 'آباد کرد' is a light-verb construction (آباد + کرد) meaning 'made prosperous/settled'. The implicit subject (he/the king) is omitted, as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s303-g266: به هر کار: 'به' (in/with/for) plus 'هر کار' ('every/each affair') sets the domain ('in every matter'). با هر کسی: 'با' here indicates toward/with regard to people ('every person/anyone'). داد کرد: the noun 'داد' (justice) combined with the light verb 'کرد' yields 'administered justice' or 'rendered judgment'; this is a common Persian collocation meaning 'acted justly' or 'dispensed justice'. The clause states a general habitual/political practice.
segments.s303-g267: همان خود: a focused/emphatic apposition — 'he himself' or 'that very one'; 'همان' adds emphasis and 'خود' is the reflexive/emphatic 'self'. و شمشیر: adds 'and sword'. و برگستوان: continues the list; this is a coordinate noun phrase (a string of items separated by 'و'). The verb that governs this list is in the surrounding context (these are objects/equipment listed).
segments.s303-g268: سپرهای چینی: 'سپرهای' is the plural 'shields' (with plural suffix -های) and 'چینی' is a post‑nominal adjective 'Chinese' (here describing the shields). و تیر و کمان: continues the list with 'arrow' and 'bow'. The whole segment is a noun phrase listing pieces of military equipment; no verb is repeated here because it is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s303-g269: ببخشید: here a past/historical third-person form meaning 'he bestowed/gave' (not the modern imperative 'forgive!'). بر لشکرش: 'بر' = 'upon/on' indicating the beneficiary, 'لشکرش' = 'his army' (لشکر + possessive suffix -ش). بی‌شمار is a post‑nominal adjective meaning 'countless/numerous'. The clause says he lavished gifts on his countless troops.
segments.s303-g270: به ویژه: a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'especially'. کسی کو کند کارزار: a relative/generic clause — 'کسی' = 'anyone/someone', 'کو' is an archaic/poetic relative marker ('who/that'), 'کند' is the present/habitual verb 'does/makes' used here to express 'who goes/engages', and 'کارزار' = 'battle/war'. Together: 'especially to anyone who goes to battle' (note Persian often uses present-tense forms to express habitual/generalizing actions even in past narrative).
segments.s303-g271: چو: subordinating conjunction 'when'. آسوده شد: 'آسوده' (at peace/relieved) + past auxiliary 'شد' = 'became relieved/was freed'. ز این: 'ز' (from) + 'این' (this) indicates cause/source. به شادی نشست: 'به' here marks manner ('in/with'), 'شادی' = 'joy/celebration', and 'نشست' is past 'sat down' (could also mean 'took a seat/held a session'); the clause describes the action that followed his relief.
segments.s303-g272: خود و جنگسازان خسرو پرست: 'خود' again emphasizes 'he himself' and 'و' coordinates with the following group. 'جنگسازان' is a plural noun meaning 'those who make war/war‑makers' (here 'warriors' or 'war‑builders'), followed by the compound modifier 'خسرو پرست' ('Khosrow‑worshipping' or 'devoted to Khosrow'), which functions adjectivally to qualify جنگسازان. Together they form the compound subject/participants who 'sat' or 'took part' as mentioned in the prior clause.
segments.s303-g161: سپه را بیارای و بیدار باش is two imperatives joined by و. سپه را: سپه (army) is the direct object marked by the definite object marker را (object/topic marker). بیارای is an imperative verb meaning “array/prepare” (classical/poetic imperative), governing سپه را. بیدار باش is a second imperative (بیدار = awake/alert + باش ‘be!’) giving an immediate admonition.
segments.s303-g162: برو خویشتن زو نگهدار باش is a sequence of imperatives. برو (go!) begins the command; خویشتن is emphatic ‘yourself’ (directing the addressee personally); ز (here appearing as زو = ز + او in classical contraction) functions as the poetic marker meaning roughly ‘of/for him’ or ‘toward him’; نگهدار باش is an imperative phrase where نگهدار (literally ‘keeper/guardian’ or verb‑like ‘keep/guard’) is followed by باش ‘be!’, so ‘be his guard/keeper yourself.’
segments.s303-g163: نوند جهاندیده شایسته بود: نوند is here a proper name or rare term (postposed modifier follows it); جهاندیده is an adjective ‘worldly‑wise’ modifying نوند; شایسته بود is the past predicative ‘was fitting/appropriate’ (شایسته followed by the past of بودن to make a past-state). Word order is subject + adjective + predicate.
segments.s303-g164: بدان راه بی‌راه بایسته بود: بدان is an archaic contraction (= به + آن) meaning ‘in/for that’; راه ‘way/course’ follows; بی‌راه is an adjective ‘wrong/unwarranted’ modifying the implied action/course; بایسته بود is the past-state predicate ‘was fitting/appropriate’ (بایسته = ‘proper/necessary’ + بود). The line expresses a paradoxical or contextual appropriateness of an improper course.
segments.s303-g165: همی رفت چون پیش رستم رسید: همی + past verb (رفت) is a habitual/continuous past sense ‘was going on’/‘kept going’; چون here functions temporally as ‘when/as’; پیش رستم رسید = ‘he reached/arrived before Rostam’ with پیش indicating presence/in front of and رسید the past of رسیدن ‘arrived/reached.’
segments.s303-g166: گو شیردل را میان بسته دید: گو is used as an epithet‑marker ‘called/named’ (classical usage: ‘so‑called’); شیردل is the epithet ‘lion‑hearted’; را marks شیردل as the direct object; میان بسته is a compound adjectival phrase ‘bound/tied in the middle’ (میان = middle, بسته = tied/closed); دید is the past ‘he saw/beheld.’
segments.s303-g167: سپه گرزها بر نهاده به دوش: سپه (the army, collective subject) with گرزها (maces) forms the object/possessed item; بر نهاده is a perfective/participial construction ‘having placed/put upon’; به دوش = ‘on (their) shoulders’ (دوش = shoulder). The participle نهاده describes the army’s preparation (they had set/slung maces on the shoulder).
segments.s303-g168: یکایک نهاده به آواز گوش: یکایک ‘one by one’ (adverbial); نهاده again is the participial ‘having put/placed’ (here describing repeated individual actions); به آواز گوش is an adverbial phrase ‘to/at the voice/sound (to the ear)’ — به used adverbially + آواز (sound/cry) + گوش (ear/hearing), i.e. ‘to the sound that (the) ear heard.’
segments.s303-g217: چو is the subordinating temporal conjunction 'when'. The verb بشنید (past) comes before its objects in this poetic order; گفتارها is the plural direct object 'the speeches/words' and شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the object (a common poetic inversion).
segments.s303-g218: خوش آمدش is an idiomatic past construction: خوش 'pleasing' + آمدش ('it came to him') = 'it pleased him' / 'he was pleased.' The enclitic -ش on آمد marks 'to him.' و simply links clauses. ایمن شد uses the past of شدن 'to become' (شد) with ایمن 'safe' and از روزگار 'from fate/fortune' to mean 'became secure from the fortunes of life.'
segments.s303-g219: بیامد is the past 'he came.' به دلشاد is a locative/descrptive phrase 'in a cheerful mood' (به + adjective). به بهشت uses به + place meaning 'into/toward paradise,' and گنگ is a postpositive adjective 'mute' or 'muffled,' describing the paradise as hushed — a poetic epithet.
segments.s303-g220: ابا here is a verb meaning 'to refuse' or 'to be unwilling' with an omitted subject (the king). Its objects follow: آلت لشکر 'the implements/arsenal of the army' (آلت head noun + لشکر as possessor) and ساز جنگ 'the instruments/tunes of war' — two coordinated objects linked by و.
segments.s303-g221: همی is a durative/adverbial particle adding continuous or habitual aspect ('kept on/was continually'). گشت here means 'went/roamed.' بر گرد آن شارستان is the prepositional phrase 'around that town' (بر گرد = 'around'). The verb plus همی gives a repeated/persistent action.
segments.s303-g222: This clause contains the negated perception: ندید 'he did not see.' به دستی functions idiomatically as 'not a hand' / 'no one at hand' (دستی 'at hand' / 'a hand, a person'), and اندر او is the locative 'in it' (او referring back to the town). خارستان is the predicate 'a thorny thicket' describing the place. Word order puts the negated verb with its object before the locative phrase.
segments.s303-g223: یکی کاخ بودش is an existential construction 'there was a certain palace' (یکی = 'a certain'). بودش is the past 'it was' with enclitic -ش. سر اندر هوا 'its head/top in the air' is a descriptive locative phrase meaning the palace's summit towered into the sky; سر (top) precedes the locative اندر هوا.
segments.s303-g224: برآورده is a past/adjectival participle 'raised/erected' (here 'erected'); it modifies the palace. شاه فرمان روا is an appositive/title 'the king, the sovereign' (فرمان‌روا is a compound epithet). The agent is expressed by juxtaposition rather than an explicit agent preposition.
segments.s303-g121: نخستین is an adverb/adjectival particle meaning “first.” که here is a subordinating conjunction with a temporal/causal sense (≈ “when/that”). از is the preposition “from.” کلک is the object and here means “reed‑pen” (the writing implement). بگشاد is an archaic past form (ب‑ prefix + گشاد) meaning “opened/released”; it functions as the main past verb of the subordinate clause. شست is a noun meaning “thumb” and is the object being released from the pen; word order is poetic (verb before object) but the clause reads “when he opened/released the thumb from the pen.”
segments.s303-g122: قراخان is the subject (proper name/title). ز is the poetic preposition “from.” پیکان (in the plural form here) is the noun “arrows.” رستم is the possessor (Rostam) modifying پیکان. بخست is a past tense verb (3rd singular) from بستن/بخستن in the poetic register meaning “shut/closed” or here more contextually “took shelter/closed himself off”; the clause reads roughly “Qara Khan (did) close himself from Rostam’s arrows.”
segments.s303-g123: به introduces a locative/relational phrase (here “in/to”); توران زمین is the compound meaning “the land of Turan.” شد is the simple past of شدن “to become.” کنون is an adverb “now.” کینه‌خواه is a predicate adjective/noun meaning “avenger” or “vengeful (one).” Together: “Now the land of Turan became vengeful.”
segments.s303-g124: همانا is an emphatic interjection “verily/indeed.” که here introduces the subordinate clause (≈ “that”). آگاهی is the noun “information/news.” آمد is the simple past of آمدن “came” and here means “reached/arrived.” به شاه is the indirect object “to the king.” The clause therefore means “verily, that information came to the king.”
segments.s303-g125: به شادی is an adverbial phrase “in joy/with joy.” به لشکر is another locative phrase “upon/among the army/host.” بر آمد is the compound verb (بر + آمد) meaning “rose up” or “arose.” خروش is the noun “cry/shout/uproar,” the thing that rose; poetic word order places the verb before the noun. The phrase reads “a cry rose up among the host in joy.”
segments.s303-g126: سپهدار ترکان is the nominative phrase “the commander of the Turks” (title + genitive). همی is an archaic continuous/iterative particle meaning “kept on / was still / continually.” داشت here is past tense of داشتن used in a periphrastic sense “kept/held” and with گوش (ear/attention) forms the idiom “kept ear” = “listened/pay(ed) attention.” گوش here is not just the literal ear but “attention.”
segments.s303-g127: هر آن کس که is a relative/indefinite expression meaning “every one who / whoever.” بودند is the past plural form of بودن “to be” (here agreeing with the implied plural sense of “those people”). خسروپرست is the predicate or appositive meaning “devoted to the king / king‑worshipper,” so the clause reads “everyone who was devoted to the king.”
segments.s303-g128: به شادی و رامش are adverbial nouns “in joy and tranquility.” گشادند is the past plural (they) of the verb (بگشادن / گشودن in poetic register) meaning “opened.” دست is the direct object “hands.” The phrase therefore means “they opened their hands in joy and peace” (a formulaic way to express joyful action/support).
segments.s303-g57: نماند is a past negative verb meaning 'was not left / remained not' and comes before the noun phrase (verb–subject order). یکی ز این سواران به جای is a noun phrase 'one of these riders in place' with ز marking the partitive 'of' and به جای indicating 'in his place / at his post'. The whole segment means 'not one of these riders remained at his post.'
segments.s303-g58: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily / truly'. سپهر is the subject 'the sky / heavens'. اندر آید ز پای is an older poetic construction: اندر آید 'comes down / descends' and ز پای 'from its footing' (literally 'from the foot'), together meaning 'the heavens would fall down.'
segments.s303-g59: ز بس here is causal 'from / because of so much'; چاک چاک is a reduplicated adjective 'riven, gaping' describing تبرزین 'the battle-axe'. و خود links to what follows, 'and himself' (or 'and his own [soul]'). The phrase emphasizes the violent chopping of the axe and then points to the reflexive subject.
segments.s303-g60: روانها is the plural subject 'the souls/spirits'. همی is the archaic continuous particle meaning 'continually / kept on'. داد is the past of دادن 'gave' and takes تن را (object marked by را) — 'gave the body' — and درود here means 'a greeting / farewell'. Grammatically: 'the souls continually gave the body a farewell.'
segments.s303-g61: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. کیخسرو is the proper name (the subject). آن پیچش جنگ is the object phrase 'that twist/turn of the battle' (پیچش governing جنگ). دید is the past verb 'saw / beheld'. The clause reads 'When Kay Khosrow saw that turn of battle...'.
segments.s303-g62: جهان is used as 'this life / the world of existence' and is the subject of the predicate bracketing تنگ دید. بر دل خویشتن is a locative/experiential phrase 'upon his own heart' (بر = upon, خویشتن = himself). تنگ دید literally 'saw narrow' = 'felt (the world) become narrow upon his heart' — an idiom meaning he felt constrained or anxious.
segments.s303-g63: بیامد is the past 'came'. به یکسو means 'to one side / aside'. ز پشت سپاه is a source phrase 'from the rear of the army' (ز = from, پشت = rear, سپاه = host). Word order places the verb first, then the directional and source phrases: 'he came aside from the army's rear.'
segments.s303-g64: به پیش here means 'into the presence of / before'. خداوند in this context is 'the lord / sovereign' (not the divine sense). شد is the past auxiliary 'became', and دادخواه is a predicate noun 'seeker of justice / petitioner'. Together: 'he went before the lord and became a seeker of justice.'
segments.s303-g193: کسی را (kasi ra) uses را as the definite/object marker after کسی ("someone"); the relative clause begins with که and the verb خواند appears at the end, a common Classical Persian order: 'he called/summoned someone who was near.' نزدیک بد پیش is a small phrasal bundle where پیش ("before, in the presence of") attaches to خواند (پیش خواند = 'called forward / brought before') and بد is an adjective qualifying the situation/person as 'bad/ill/hostile.'
segments.s303-g194: وز ایشان here functions like و از (classical contraction) meaning 'and from them' or 'and of them'; فراوان modifies سخنها (adverbially 'much/many'), and سخنها is the plural 'words/speeches.' راند (from راندن) follows the object and in this poetic usage means to 'send/let forth' or 'utter/drive (forth)' words — verb at the end as usual in Persian.
segments.s303-g195: بپرسید is a past tense/simple perfect 'asked' (verb placed before its subordinate clause here). کاین را چه بینید روی is an embedded question: کاین را ('this/that' with object marker را) + چه ('what') + بینید ('you/they see' — here 'see') + روی ('face') — literally 'what do you see of this face?'. Word order places the verb بینید before the final noun روی in this poetic line but the clause is a direct question reported by the narrator.
segments.s303-g196: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said.' با here is the preposition 'with/to' introducing the addressee or companion; نامور چاره‌جوی is a compound epithet 'the famed (نامور) seeker of remedy/solution (چاره‌جوی)'. Note گفْت is used as the narrative verb 'said' in past narrative position.
segments.s303-g197: This independent clause begins with که ('that') introducing a content clause. در + دژ marks location 'in the fortress'; گنگ is the fortress-name/descriptor and here functions as a proper name (poetic/place name) rather than the common adjective 'mute' in this context; آن همه گنج شاه = 'all that treasure of the king' (آن همه = 'that much/all of').
segments.s303-g198: چه بایست اکنون همه رنج راه is a rhetorical question: چه بایست = 'what ought/what need is there' (بایست as modal 'must/ought'); اکنون 'now'; همه رنج راه = 'all the hardships of the road' (compound noun phrase, with رنج 'hardship' modified by راه 'of the road'). The verb/auxiliary sense is modal rather than physical 'stand.'
segments.s303-g199: زمین هشت فرسنگ بالای اوی states a measurement: زمین 'the land' + هشت فرسنگ 'eight farsangs/parasangs' + بالای اوی 'above it' (بالای = 'above, atop'; اوی here = 'it' referring back to the fortress/place). Word order places the quantity immediately after the measured noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s303-g200: همانا که is an emphatic formula 'verily/for indeed that'; چارست is an archaic adjectival numeral 'is four/fourfold' describing پهنای اوی 'its breadth' (پهنای = 'width/breadth', اوی = 'its'). The clause asserts the breadth (probably in the same unit) after the previous height measurement.
segments.s303-g73: همانگه is an adverbial phrase 'at that moment.' برآمد is a past-tense verb 'arose/issued forth.' The noun phrase یکی باد سخت follows in somewhat inverted, emphatic order: یکی 'a certain' + باد 'wind' + سخت 'strong/violent' (postpositive adjective), so literally 'a certain strong wind arose.'
segments.s303-g74: که is the relative/introducing conjunction 'that/which.' بشکست is a past (3rd‑sg) verb 'broke' (classical/past form with initial ب-). شاداب is an adjective 'fresh, vigorous' modifying شاخ 'bough/branch,' and درخت 'tree' follows as a genitive construction (شاخِ درخت = 'the tree's bough').
segments.s303-g75: همی here marks iterative/ongoing action ('kept' / continuous). خاک is the subject 'dust.' بر داشت is a verbal compound (بر + داشت) meaning 'lifted/rose up' — همی ... داشت gives a progressive sense 'dust was rising.' از رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the battlefield.'
segments.s303-g76: بزد is a past (3rd‑sg) verb 'struck' or 'smote.' بر رخ = 'upon the face' (بر + رخ, marking the target). شاه is 'the king' (object/possessed face), and توران سپاه (word order inverted from modern Persian) denotes 'the army/host of Turan' as the agent/subject performing the action; classical/poetic word order often places the subject after verb for emphasis.
segments.s303-g77: کسی 'someone/anyone.' کو functions as an archaic relative/particle here ('who/that' in older Persian idiom). سر از جنگ برتافتی is a fixed expression: سر 'head' + از جنگ 'from the battle' + برتافتی (an older past form meaning 'came away/escaped/emerged'), so the whole means 'whoever got his head/escaped from the battle.' Note the second‑person poetic/formal ending in برتافتی used generically.
segments.s303-g78: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiab.' آگهی is the object 'news/information,' and یافتی is the past (3rd‑sg) verb 'received/found' — 'when Afrasiab received the news.'
segments.s303-g79: بریدی is a 2nd‑person past 'you cut' (narrative/direct address). به خنجر is an instrumental phrase 'with a dagger' (به used here as 'by/with'), سرش را is the definite object 'his head' with the object marker را, and ز تن is a poetic variant of از تن 'from the body' indicating the head was severed from the body.
segments.s303-g80: جز از = 'except from/other than.' خاک و ریگش = 'earth and his sand' (ریگ with possessive suffix referring to the grave's sand). نبودی is a negative past-form 'there would not be / was not' (classical conditional/optative flavor here), and کفن is the predicate 'shroud' — together meaning 'there would be no shroud for him except earth and sand.'
segments.s303-g241: وگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'); خود emphasises the subject ('he himself'); نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not come' in this conditional; فرستد is a present/future-oriented form 'sends/ will send' (dispatches) and سپاه is the object 'an army'. Word order is cinematic/poetic: condition first, then the action promised.
segments.s303-g242: کز is the relative/connector 'which/that' introducing the clause describing the army; این‌سو 'this way/hither' marks direction; خرامد is an archaic present form 'strides/proudly marches' (classical style); همی is the old habitual/progressive particle (adds ongoing or emphatic sense); کینه خواه is a compound meaning 'seeking vengeance' where خواه is the verb 'wants/desires' used adjectivally.
segments.s303-g243: فرستاده is a noun 'messenger' (literally 'sent one'); از نزد combines the preposition 'from' (از) with نزد 'in the presence of / from the court of' to indicate origin or source ('from Afrasiab'); افراسیاب is the proper name.
segments.s303-g244: به ... اندر آمد is a motion phrase 'entered into / came into' (به + اندر + آمد), placing the messenger at the destination (چین here as a place name); به هنگام is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time of' and خواب 'sleep' gives the timing ('during sleep' or 'while (someone) slept'). Word order puts destination then the temporal modifier.
segments.s303-g245: سرافراز is an honorific adjective 'exalted / lofty' modifying فغفور (the title for the emperor); بنواختش is a poetic verb 'played/struck it' (here likely musical or percussive action) with the clitic -ش referring back to the thing struck.
segments.s303-g246: یکی introduces 'one/a certain' followed by خرم 'joyful' modifying ایوان 'iwan/portico, hall'; بپرداختش (past form) means 'paid it' or 'returned it' — in poetic usage 'answered/echoed it.' The clause describes a responsive sound or rejoicing in the hall.
segments.s303-g247: وز آن‌سو is an archaic connective phrase 'and/from that side' (وز is an older conjunction/prepositional form); به introduces a locative phrase; گنگ here is an adjective 'muffled/unclear' describing the state or atmosphere; اندر is the preposition 'in/within'; افراسیاب is the subject name. The whole phrase places Afrasiab on the opposite side in a muffled/worried state.
segments.s303-g248: نه ... نه is the standard 'neither ... nor' construction. آرام بودش means 'he was not calm' (بودش = 'was' with enclitic -ش referring to him); خورد 'ate' and خواب 'sleep' form the coordinated verbs 'neither ate nor slept' describing his restless condition. Verbs are in past tense, recounting his state.
segments.s303-g81: چنین تا is here a short sequence meaning 'thus, until' where چنین (an adverb) sets the mood 'thus/so' and تا introduces the temporal clause 'until'. سپهر و زمین تار شد is a verbless-to-verb sequence where تار شد = 'became dark'; سپهر (sky) and زمین (earth) are coordinated subjects. Note that تار here means 'dark' in context (poetic usage).
segments.s303-g82: فراوان ز ترکان گرفتار شد is a short passive clause: فراوان qualifies the (implicit) people 'many/much', ز ترکان marks the agent/source 'by/from the Turks' (poetic ز = by/from), گرفتار شد is a passive construction 'were captured/afflicted' where گرفتـار is the participial adjective and شد functions as the past auxiliary forming the passive.
segments.s303-g83: بر آمد is a compound verb meaning 'arose/came up' (بر as a verbal prefix + آمد). شب is the subject 'night'. The coordinate phrase چادرِ مشک رنگ is a noun phrase 'a tent (or veil) of musk‑color' where مشک modifies رنگ (musk‑colored). Word order is verb then subject for poetic emphasis, then the following noun phrase.
segments.s303-g84: بپوشید is a past/perfective 3rd‑person form meaning 'covered/put on' formed with the preverb ب- plus پوشید (classical/poetic usage). تا introduces a purposive clause 'so that/in order that'; کس نیاید به جنگ uses نیاید (negative present/subjunctive) to express purpose/negative desire 'that no one should come to battle', with به جنگ = 'to battle'.
segments.s303-g85: سپه باز چیدند شاهان is an inverted, poetic word order: سپه (the host) + باز (again) + چیدند (they arranged/set) + شاهان (the kings). Chیدند is the past tense 'they arranged/arrayed', and شاهان is the agent (subject) placed after the verb for stylistic effect; ز دشت means 'from the plain'.
segments.s303-g86: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'. روی زمین is a genitive phrase 'the surface/face of the earth' (روی = surface/face + زمین = earth). ز آسمان marks source/instrument 'from the sky', تیره گشت is the verb phrase 'became dark' (تیره = dark, گشت = became/turned). The clause describes a simultaneous change of darkness coming from above.
segments.s303-g87: همه دامن کوه تا پیش رود is a noun phrase of extent: همه = 'the whole', دامن کوه = 'the skirt/slope (foot) of the mountain', تا = 'as far as/up to', پیش رود = 'the front of the river' or 'the river before it' (پیش here 'in front of/before', رود = river). Grammatically تا marks the limit of extent.
segments.s303-g88: سپه بود با جوشن و درع و خود is a predicative clause: سپه بود = 'the host was' (بود is past of 'to be'), با introduces accompaniment 'with', جوشن and درع are types of armor ('joshun' cuirass/scale armor, 'dar' can mean shield/armor here), and خود 'themselves/their own' emphasizes the men themselves. The sequence lists the army's equipment and presence.
segments.s303-g129: سواری (subject) + بیامد (past verb) is a simple past clause: 'a rider came.' هم is a particle meaning 'also' (attached here after the verb for emphasis). اندر شتاب is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in haste' (اندر = 'in/within', شتاب = 'haste'). Word order is subject–verb then particles/adjuncts, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s303-g130: خروشان is a participial/adverbial adjective describing the manner of approach ('roaring/tumultuously'). به نزدیک is a directional phrase 'toward the vicinity of' (به = 'towards/into', نزدیک = 'near'). افراسیاب is the proper name being approached. The whole group modifies or completes the preceding clause ('came ... roaring toward Afrasiab').
segments.s303-g131: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('that/for'). از لشکر ما is a prepositional phrase 'from our host/army' (از = 'from', لشکر = 'army', ما = 'our/us'). قراخان is the agent/name. برست is an archaic past form meaning 'broke away / departed' (here: left the host). Word order is prepositional phrase + subject + archaic past verb.
segments.s303-g132: رسیدست is an older/perfective form of رسیدن ('has arrived'); the suffix -ست is part of the fixed poetic form. نزدیک ما is the locative adverbial 'near us'. مرد is an indefinite noun 'a man'. شست is the past of شستن ('he washed'); here it follows the noun and likely describes the man's recent action (literally 'a man washed' — understood as 'a man who had washed himself' in context). The clause therefore reports someone arriving near the speaker and having washed.
segments.s303-g133: سپاهی is 'army/host' (here collective). به توران is the directional phrase 'toward Turan'. نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'to place/set'; in the idiom روی نهادن it means 'to set one's face' or 'to turn (one's) course' — hence 'they turned toward Turan'. روی functions as the object of نهادند in this idiomatic expression.
segments.s303-g134: کز (= که + ز) introduces a result/relative clause 'from which / because of them'. ایشان = 'they/them'. شود is the present/subjunctive/inchoative auxiliary 'becomes / is made'. ناپدید is an adjectival past-participle 'vanished / gone'. آب جوی is a possessive noun phrase 'the water of the stream' (آب = 'water', جوی = 'stream'). The construction means that, on their account, the stream's water was made to disappear (hyperbolic: they so crowded it or so drained it).
segments.s303-g135: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the speech verb. گفت here is the speech verb 'said' (the sense list given is dialog-related; the intended meaning in context is 'he said'). با + رایزن شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'with the king's counselor/advisor' (با = 'with', رایزن = 'advisor', شهریار = 'king') — i.e., the speaker is the royal advisor or he spoke with the advisor. Word order places the manner first, then the speech verb and its complements.
segments.s303-g136: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. پیکار سخت is a noun phrase 'a hard/severe battle' (پیکار = 'battle', سخت = 'severe'). اندر آمد is an idiomatic past expression 'came in / came upon' (اندر = 'in', آمد = 'came'), and به کار 'to the affair / into play' completes the idiom meaning 'came into action' or 'came to pass'. Together they state that a fierce fight has arisen.
segments.s303-g177: به رسم مهان: a prepositional phrase 'به رسم' (according to the custom/rite) followed by 'مهان' (guests/hosts) specifying which custom. 'کشته را دخمه کرد' has an omitted subject (he/they); 'کشته' (the slain) is marked by the object/definite marker 'را'; 'دخمه کرد' is a verbal phrase meaning 'made/placed (in) a tomb' with 'کرد' as the simple past of 'to do/make'.
segments.s303-g178: چو برداشت زان خاک و خون نبرد: 'چو' = 'when'. 'برداشت' here is the past action 'he lifted/removed' (sense = lifting/removal). 'زان' = 'from that/therefrom'. 'خاک و خون' are the materials 'earth and blood'. 'نبرد' is ambiguous here in the manuscript (likely an archaic verbal form) and does not match the provided nominal sense list, so it is left without a matching index.
segments.s303-g179: بنه بر نهاد و سپه بر نشاند: subject is omitted (he). 'بنه' here functions as a small bundle/pack (i.e. baggage) and is the thing put down; 'بر نهاد' is a verbal compound meaning 'placed upon/put down' (نهاد = placed). The conjunction 'و' links the clauses; 'سپه' = 'army/host', and 'بر نشاند' = 'he set/installed (the army)', with نشاند as an archaic past form meaning 'caused to sit/placed'.
segments.s303-g180: دمان از پس شاه ترکان براند: 'دمان' literally 'hem/skirt' used figuratively for the immediate vicinity; 'از پس' = 'from behind/after' (here 'from the rear/close presence of'); 'شاه' is the king; 'ترکان' is the Turks (subject); 'براند' is a verbal form meaning 'drove away' (to drive off). Word order is typical classical Persian where verb follows its object/phrases and the subject (ترکان) appears before the verb.
segments.s303-g181: چو نزدیک شهر آمد افراسیاب: 'چو' = 'when'. 'نزدیک شهر آمد' = 'came near the city' (adverb 'نزدیک' before the noun 'شهر', then the past verb 'آمد'). 'افراسیاب' is the subject appearing after the verb, a common classical order.
segments.s303-g182: بر آن بد که رستم شود سیرخواب: 'بر آن بد' is an idiomatic construction meaning 'it was bad/ill concerning that'—i.e. he was anxious/ troubled at the thought. 'که' introduces the subordinate clause. 'رستم شود' uses شود ('become') expressing possibility or fear 'that Rostam might become/enter ...'. 'سیرخواب' is the predicate, a noun meaning 'sound/deep sleep'.
segments.s303-g183: کنون من شبیخون کنم بر سرش: 'کنون' = 'now'. Subject 'من' (I) is explicit. 'شبیخون کنم' uses the present/subjunctive of 'کردن' to express intention 'I will make/do a night-raid'. 'بر سرش' is a prepositional phrase 'upon his head' (ش is the possessive suffix 'his'), a figurative locution meaning directed at him.
segments.s303-g184: برآریم گرد از سر لشکرش: 'برآریم' is a first-person-plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us raise/bring' (here 'let us surround/bring around'). 'گرد' functions adverbially 'around/about'. 'از سر لشکرش' literally 'from the head of his army' — i.e. from the front/leader of his host; the phrase as a whole means 'let us encircle the head/front of his army.'
segments.s303-g153: بدانست is a 3rd-person past verb ('he knew/realized') placed before its subject, a common poetic order. خسرو is the subject following the verb. که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the subordinate clause سالار چین ('the commander of China'), where سالار (commander) is followed by its specifier چین (China).
segments.s303-g154: چرا is the interrogative 'why' (here functioning as an indirect question inside the 'that' clause). رفت is the simple past 'he left/went.' بیگاه is an adverb meaning 'at dawn' or 'early morning' and is placed adjacent to the verb in poetic word order. زان دشت کین is a prepositional phrase: زان = 'from/away from that', دشت = 'plain', and کین = 'vengeance/hatred', so together 'from that plain of vengeance.'
segments.s303-g155: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' The following coordinated nouns (گستهم و رستم) are the sources of the news (i.e., 'from Gostaham and Rostam'); خبر یافتست is a compound past: خبر (news) + یافتست ('he received/found'), with یافتست as 3rd-person past. Note: گستهم here is a proper name in context; the provided sense list did not include that reading.
segments.s303-g156: بدان here functions as 'on that / with that' (ب + دان in an archaic/poetic fused form) introducing آگهی ('information/news'). تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swift/quick' modifying the verb that follows. بشتافتست is the 3rd-person past 'he hastened/ran' (literary past form). Note: دان is acting as a demonstrative/relational element in 'بدان آگهی' rather than the literal verb 'to know,' so its dictionary senses do not match exactly.
segments.s303-g157: نوندی is a noun (here likely a proper or technical term — possibly the name of a tune/place) used in the sentence's opening; the exact sense is rare/poetic. برافگند is a past verb 'he cast/put out/installed' or 'he spread/dispersed' (literary). هم = 'also' or 'too.' در زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' or 'in that moment' (در = 'in/at', زمان = 'time/moment').
segments.s303-g158: فرستاد is the past 'he sent/ dispatched' (here with purposeful sense). نزدیک functions adverbially/prepositionally as 'near' or 'to the vicinity of.' رستم is the name 'Rostam.' دمان is a noun meaning 'skirt/hem' or metaphorically 'edge/side' (i.e., 'near Rostam's side/camp'); together نزدیک رستم دمان = 'near Rostam's skirt/edge' (poetic way to say near Rostam).
segments.s303-g159: که is the complementizer/relative 'that' linking the report of the message. برگشت is the past 'he returned' (3rd-person). ز این کینه uses ز in a causal/ablative sense 'because of / from this' and این کینه = 'this hatred/grudge.' افراسیاب, the subject, follows the verb in the poetic order: 'Afrasiab returned because of this grudge.'
segments.s303-g160: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly.' به جنگ تو دارد شتاب is a clause where به جنگ تو = 'to/against you in battle' (به = 'toward/for', جنگ = 'battle', تو = 'you'), and دارد شتاب literally 'he has haste' (دارد 'has', شتاب 'haste'), a periphrastic expression meaning 'he is hastening' or 'he is making haste' to wage war on you.
segments.s303-g145: سراسر همه لشکر is an emphatic adverbial phrase: سراسر ('throughout') + همه ('all') + لشکر ('the host/army') = 'throughout the whole army'. دید is the past 3rd-person singular of دیدن ('saw, perceived'). رای is the object meaning 'plan/counsel'. Poetic word order puts the verb (دید) immediately before its object (رای).
segments.s303-g146: همان مرد فرزانه و رهنمای is a noun phrase identifying the person: همان ('that/the same') points back to a previously mentioned figure; مرد ('man') is the head noun; فرزانه ('wise') and رهنمای ('guide/adviser') are postpositive adjectives/epithets joined by و. The phrase labels the adviser seen by the host.
segments.s303-g147: بنه هرچه بودش هم آنجا بماند: بنه ('bundle, small heap') + هرچه ('whatever') marks an indefinite quantity. بودش is the past form of بودن with the enclitic -ش ('it/there'), literally 'it was'. هم ('also/even') emphasizes that these things too stayed. آنجا ('there') is locative and بماند is the 3rd-person singular subjunctive/jussive of ماندن used narratively to mean 'remained'.
segments.s303-g148: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. آتش ('fire') is the subject; از آن دشت ('from that plain') is a prepositional phrase (از + آن + دشت). لشکر here is the object of the verb راندن in the sense 'the army (was) driven'. براند is the 3rd-person singular present/past-poetic of راندن used narratively as 'drove away'. The clause means 'when fire drove the host from that plain.'
segments.s303-g149: همانگه ('at that very moment') is an adverbial temporal expression. طلایه ('vanguard') is the subject and بیامد is the past 3rd-person singular of آمدن in an older/poetic form meaning 'came'. ز ('from', poetic form of از) introduces the origin, دشت ('plain').
segments.s303-g150: که introduces a subordinate clause describing what accompanied that arrival ('for/as/that'). گرد سپاه is a genitive-like phrase where گرد ('dust, cloud of dust') qualifies سپاه ('the host/army') — 'a dust-cloud of the army'. از هوا برگذشت: از هوا ('through the air') + برگذشت (past 3rd-person of گذشتن, 'passed by/over'). The clause says that a dust-cloud from the army passed through the air.
segments.s303-g151: همه دشت ('the whole plain') is the subject. خرگاه و خیمه است is a nominal predicate: خرگاه ('stalls/low shelters') and خیمه ('tents') linked by و, with است 'is'. بس ('enough' / 'that is all') functions as a resumptive particle meaning 'and that was all' or 'merely so.'
segments.s303-g152: از ایشان ('of them' / 'from those [people]') specifies whose relation is meant. به خیمه درون ('into the tents' with درون 'inside') gives the location. نیست کس is a negative existential: نیست ('is not / there is not') + کس ('anyone/no one'), so together 'there was nobody inside the tents.' Note the negative predicate precedes کس, yielding 'no one exists.'
segments.s303-g41: زمین is the subject 'the earth'. کان here is the comparative/semblative 'as if' (classical usage). آهن شد is a simple past copular construction 'became iron'. از + noun phrase (از میخ نعل) marks cause/source: 'from the nails of horseshoes'. Word order is poetic but straightforward: subject — comparative marker — predicate — causal phrase.
segments.s303-g42: همه آب دریا is a quantified noun phrase 'the whole/entire water of the sea' (همه before the noun). شد is the past/copular 'became'. از introduces the cause/material 'from blood', and لعل qualifies خون as ruby-like ('ruby blood' / 'blood that makes it ruby-colored'). The clause states result (became) + cause (از...).
segments.s303-g43: به سر بر is a locative phrase 'upon the head' (به + noun + بر for poetic locative 'on the head'). ز introduces source/material: ز گرد سیاه ابر = 'from/the dust of a black cloud' where گرد is used as 'dust'. بست is the main past verb 'he bound/tied'. The object of بست appears in the following segment (typical enjambment).
segments.s303-g44: تبیره is the direct object of the previous بست (i.e. 'he bound/tied a tabīre/turban'); دل سنگ خارا is a noun phrase 'a heart of stone, flinty' with سنگ خارا postpositively modifying دل; بخست is the past 'he shut/closed' (here applied to the heart: 'he closed his heart with flinty stone'). Note: تبیره in this context functions as a head-cover (object of bind) rather than the listed sense 'interpretation'.
segments.s303-g45: زمین is subject and گشت is the past 'became/turned'. چون is the comparative 'like/as', introducing the simile چادر آبنوس 'a tent of ebony' (noun + adjective), so the clause reads 'the earth became like an ebony tent.'
segments.s303-g46: ستاره is subject; غمی (a noun) with شد ('became') forms 'became sorrow/grief' — i.e. 'grew sorrowful'. ز introduces the cause 'because of' and آوای کوس is a genitive 'the sound/voice of the drum'.
