The clause states result (became sorrowful) followed by cause (from the drum's sound).
segments.s303-g47: زمین (the earth) with گشت ('became') plus the participial/attributive جنب ان 'moving/agit­ated' yields 'the earth became moving' or 'the earth was set in motion'. چو is the simile 'like/as' and ابر سیاه 'a black cloud' completes it: 'the earth moved like a black cloud.'
segments.s303-g48: تو is the pronoun 'you'. گفتی here functions as the speech verb 'you would say / you said' (the provided senses list does not include this verbal sense, so it is marked as not matched). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'always/kept on'. بر + نتابد expresses inability to bear/endure (نتابد here = 'to endure/withstand') and سپاه is the noun 'army' (subject of the inability). The clause reports repeated saying: 'you kept saying the army would not endure/stand.'
segments.s303-g257: This is a noun phrase of the form 'Noun + modifier' where each head noun carries the plural suffix -های: 'کمانهای چرخ' = 'bows of the wheel' (literally 'bows, wheel') and 'سپرهای کرگ' = 'shields of (or lined with) down'. The conjunction و links the two coordinated noun phrases; Persian places the possessor/qualifier (چرخ, کرگ) after the head noun.
segments.s303-g258: همه برجها is the subject/topic 'all the towers'. پر ز is a small adjectival/predicative phrase meaning 'full of' (پر + ز = 'full from/of'), followed by the two items خفتان و ترگ joined by و. The verb copula is omitted (typical in Persian poetry), so the idea reads 'the towers were full of X and Y.'
segments.s303-g259: گروهی ز + noun is a partitive construction: 'some of the blacksmiths' (گروهی 'a group/some', ز marking 'from among'). رنجه کرد is a past tense verbal compound meaning 'toiled/was distressed' (کرد is the auxiliary ‘did’), so the whole clause says 'some of the blacksmiths labored.'
segments.s303-g260: ز پولاد here gives the material/instrumental sense 'with/from steel'. بر هر سوی is a locative/adverbial phrase 'on/at every side', and پنجه کرد (پنجه + کرد) is 'they made/fashioned claws (or grappling-claws)'; verb آخر (کرد) appears at the end as typical SOV order in classical Persian.
segments.s303-g261: ببستند is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they bound/tied'. The prepositional phrase بر نیزه‌های دراز ('on/onto long spears'—نیزه‌های is plural with the plural marker -های and دراز modifies it) indicates the object/location to which something was bound.
segments.s303-g262: که introduces a relative/result clause. هر کس که ('whoever') is the free relative; رفتی is a classical/poetic verbal form used in such relative clauses ('whoever goes/has gone/tried to go' — context gives the modal sense). بر دژ فراز is a locative phrase 'onto the fortress summit' (بر = onto, دژ = fortress, فراز = top/high place). The clause is incomplete here and continues the consequence in the next line.
segments.s303-g263: بدان is the fused poetic preposition 'بـ + آن' = 'to/at that'. چنگ تیز is the noun 'sharp claw' (chest + adjective order: noun then adjective). اندر آویختی consists of the locative/inclusive اندر ('upon/in') plus آویختی (past/preterite form 'hung on / clung'); the construction is used impersonally/generically: 'one would be caught/cling to that sharp claw.'
segments.s303-g264: و گرنه is 'and otherwise / if not'. ز دژ is 'from the fortress' (ز = from), زود is an adverb 'quickly', and بگریختی is the past/conditional-flavored verb 'would have fled / would flee' (the b- prefix in classical Persian often marks the imperfective/potential). Together: 'and otherwise one would quickly flee from the fortress.'
segments.s303-g209: به هر گوشه‌ای is a locative phrase: به (preposition) + هر (each/every) + گوشه‌ای (corner with an indefinite/nisba -i). The noun phrase چشمه آبگیر follows as an apposition/compound (چشمه = spring, آبگیر = pond/reservoir); the copula is omitted (poetic ellipsis). Word order is typical Persian: locative phrase then the noun(s) describing what is there.
segments.s303-g210: به بالا و پهنای پرتاب تیر expresses extent: به indicates extent/limit ('up to'/'as far as'), بالا = height/above, و links to پهنای (the width/breadth). پرتاب تیر is a genitive-like compound 'the launching/flight of an arrow' that qualifies پهنای. The whole segment is an adjectival/extent phrase modifying the springs/pools (how high/wide they reach).
segments.s303-g211: همی is a poetic adverb giving a habitual/continuous sense ('ever/continually') and combines with the past verb آورد (3rd sing.) to produce a habitual past ('would bring'/'kept bringing'). موبد is the agent (a mōbed, Zoroastrian priest). از هند و روم is a simple 'from X and Y' phrase marking origin with از and coordinating و.
segments.s303-g212: بهشتی بر آورده آباد بوم is compacted/predicative: بهشتی (paradisiacal/paradise) is the theme/object, بر + آورده together form برآورده 'brought forth/raised up' (بر as verbal prefix + participial/past form آورده), and آباد بوم ('prosperous/inhabited land/homeland') states the result. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect; literal reading is 'he brought forth a paradise and made the land prosperous.'
segments.s303-g213: همانا is an emphatic/explanatory particle ('verily/indeed'). کزان is a compound relative/adverbial 'from which/from there' pointing back to a previously mentioned mound/place. باره here is poetic and functions like 'on that occasion/from that spot' (sense 'occasion/spot'), and فرسنگ names the measure (parasang); the numeral 'بیست' (twenty) is understood with فرسنگ (poetic word order may separate them).
segments.s303-g214: ببینند is 3rd‑person plural present/habitual 'they see' (here used impersonally: 'one can see'/'they can see'); آسان is an adverb 'easily'. که introduces the content clause 'who is on the plain' where بر دشت = 'on the plain' and کیست is the inverted copular interrogative 'who is'. The clause is the direct object of 'see.'
segments.s303-g215: ترا is split into the suffix ت (second‑person object) + را (the topical/definite object marker), producing 'for you'/'your'. ز این جهان = 'from this world' (ز = from). بهره = 'share/portion' and جنگست is a nominal sentence 'is war' (جنگ + copular‑stative -ست). و بس adds 'and that's enough/that's all' — a laconic summation.
segments.s303-g216: به فرجام گیتی is a temporal/locative phrase 'at/to the end of the world' (به marking point/limit). نماند is the verb 'does not remain/there will not remain' (3rd sing. with negative prefix), and به کس 'for anyone' (here in negative context meaning 'no one'). The phrase states the final universality: no one will be left by the world's end.
segments.s303-g65: که‌ای is a vocative/relative address (که + ای) meaning “O who…”; برتر از is the comparative construction ‘superior to/greater than’ and takes a following phrase introduced by از; دانش پارسا (‘knowledge’ + ‘pious’) is a noun phrase where پارسا qualifies دانش (‘the pious’/‘the pious in learning’), so the whole segment reads “O who are superior in knowledge to the pious.”
segments.s303-g66: جهاندار is a nominative/title ‘world‑ruler’ or sovereign; و simply connects the two predicates; بر here means ‘over/against’ and governs the following noun phrase هر کسی (‘every/any person’), while پادشا (archaic form of پادشاه) is the predicative noun ‘king.’ The order is poetic but means “and king over every man.”
segments.s303-g67: اگر introduces a conditional clause; نیستم is the first‑person present negative of بودن (‘I am not’) and appears before the subject من in typical verb–subject poetic order; ستم یافته is a compound where یافته (past participle ‘having received/found’) modifies ستم to mean ‘one who has suffered injustice/been wronged.’ Together: ‘If I am not one who has been wronged…’.
segments.s303-g68: چو is the simile marker ‘like/as’; آهن is the noun ‘iron’; به + کوره درون gives the locative ‘into/in the inner furnace’ (به here = ‘into/towards’); تافته is a poetic past participle meaning ‘baked/forged/tempered’ (an archaic/poetic usage). The phrase describes being conditioned like iron in a furnace.
segments.s303-g69: نخواهم is the first‑person negative of خواه(دن) ‘I do not want/I will not’; that is followed by که introducing a subordinate clause; پیروز is a predicative adjective ‘victorious’; باشم is the present subjunctive of بودن (‘that I be’), used after نخواهم که; به جنگ is a prepositional phrase ‘in battle.’ So: ‘I do not wish that I be victorious in battle.’
segments.s303-g70: نه begins the negative coordination ‘nor’; the clause’s verb is کنم with a preverbal بر that forms a causative/operative sense (‘do to / impose upon’), and دادگر is the affected party ‘the just one’; جای تنگ (‘a narrow/cramped place’) is the result being imposed. Overall: ‘nor will I make the just man’s place cramped,’ i.e. I will not oppress a just person.
segments.s303-g71: بگفت is the simple past ‘he said’ (ب‑ prefixed classical past); این is the direct object ‘this’; و connects to the next action; بر خاک (preposition + object) means ‘on the ground’; مالید is the past tense ‘he rubbed’ and روی is the direct object ‘(his) face.’ Word order is poetic: ‘he said this and rubbed his face on the ground.’
segments.s303-g72: جهان پر شد is a copular/result clause ‘the world became full’; از introduces the source/reason (‘with/from’); ناله زار means ‘piteous lament/wailing’ (ناله = lament, زار = piteous/wretched); اوی is an archaic clitic meaning ‘his/her/of him,’ so the whole phrase reads ‘the world was filled with his piteous lament.’
segments.s303-g137: چو is a conditional/time conjunction here meaning 'when' or 'if'. رستم is the subject. بگیرد (present/subjunctive form of گرفتن with the archaic prefixed ب-) is the verb 'seize/take' referring to a potential action by Rostam. سر گاه ما is the object phrase: سر here means 'head' or 'chief' and گاه is used poetically to indicate a place/post (literally 'place' or 'position'), together meaning 'the head/chief place of our post' or 'the head of our camp.' Word order is subject–verb–object in this clause, with the subordinate conditional introduced by چو.
segments.s303-g138: به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase 'suddenly' (به functioning as the marker that forms the adverbial). گم شود is a passive/impersonal construction: گم (lost) + شود (become) → 'become lost' (here predicting the result). راه ما is the affected noun phrase 'our way/path.' The clause states the consequent result of the preceding conditional.
segments.s303-g139: کنونش is کنون ('now') with an enclitic -ش that gives a sense of 'his/its' or 'at this moment (for him)' and functions to introduce what follows as his present thought. گمان means 'thought/belief.' آن‌که is the complementizer/relative 'that/which' introducing the subordinate clause. ما نشنویم is a negative present-subjunctive/optative construction 'we do not hear / we should not hear' (ن– prefix marks negative, شن- root + -ویم first-person plural ending). Together: 'Now his belief is that we should not hear.'
segments.s303-g140: چنین کار means 'such a deed' or 'deeds like this.' در جنگ is the locative phrase 'in/at the war.' کیخسرویم is کیخسرو + the 1st‑person plural ending -یم, here idiomatically meaning 'we belong to Kay Khosrow' or 'we are (fighters) of Kay Khosrow' — i.e., these sorts of actions pertain to (or are the business of) Kay Khosrow's war. The clause characterizes whose war or to whom such deeds belong.
segments.s303-g141: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause 'when/if.' آتش ('fire') is the simile core: 'like fire.' بر ایشان is the prepositional phrase 'upon them/against them.' شبیخون ('night-raid') names the mode of attack and functions adverbially here. کنیم is the present-subjunctive/imperative first-person-plural of کردن 'we do/make' — used to mean 'if we do/make (this attack)'. Word order places the verb at the end, typical of Persian subordinate clauses.
segments.s303-g142: ز خون ('with/from blood') indicates the means/result: the country will be stained by blood. روی کشور literally 'the face/surface of the country' means 'across the land.' چو introduces a simile 'like' before جیحون (the Jayhūn/Amu Darya river). کنیم is again 'we will make' (we will cause the country's face to be like the Jayhūn in its flow of blood). The whole clause says the country will run with blood like a river.
segments.s303-g143: چو introduces the temporal clause 'when/if.' کیخسرو آید means 'Kay Khosrow comes/arrives' (آید present/subjunctive/indicative of آمدن). ز لشکر 'from/with the host' (ز = 'from'/'out of' or 'with' in poetic use; لشکر = 'army/host'). بهر here is an archaic directional/partitive marker (literally 'to/for' or 'side'), so the phrase indicates arrival with or from the army (read as 'with his host' or 'from the army on either side' in context). The clause sets the occasion for what he will see.
segments.s303-g144: نبیند is the negative present/subjunctive form 'he will not see' or 'he sees not.' مگر here means 'except' or 'but' in the sense 'he will see nothing except...' followed by a simple list بام و دیوار و شهر 'roofs and walls and the city.' The clause says that upon arrival he will see nothing else but those elements (i.e., the town in ruins or the visible built features).
segments.s303-g113: که here is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'for / since / that' introducing the clause; ما is the nominative subject 'we'; در بیابان is a locative phrase 'in the desert' placed before the verb; خبر یافتیم is the verb phrase with خبر as direct object 'news' and یافتیم (past, 1st-person plural) 'we found/learned', following Persian's verb‑final order.
segments.s303-g114: بدان is the contracted prepositional form be + ān = 'to/at that' (i.e. 'on that/at that news'); آگهی is the noun 'information/tidings'; تیز functions adverbially here 'swift/quick'; بشتافتیم is the past, 1st‑person plural of بشتافتن 'to hasten/rush' and is the main verb of the segment, placed at the end.
segments.s303-g115: شب و روز is a coordinated noun phrase 'night and day'; رستم is the topical/subject name placed after that phrase; یکی ('one') here indicates unity or sameness ('as one' / 'the same'); داشتی is the past 2nd‑person singular of داشتن 'you had/kept', with the verb finally, so the whole means roughly 'Rostam, you kept night and day as one' (i.e. you were the same/constant day and night).
segments.s303-g116: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; تنها شدی is 'you became alone' (تنها adjective + شدی past 2nd‑person singular of شدن); راه بگذاشتی is the main clause where راه 'road/way' precedes the verb بگذاشتی (past 2nd‑person), literally 'you put/laid the road' — idiomatically 'you left/abandoned the road' or 'you set out from the road' depending on context. Note the verb-final order and the temporal clause leading the sentence.
segments.s303-g117: بدیشان is the prepositional/goal phrase 'to/unto them' (به + ایشان); رسیدیم is past 1st‑person plural 'we arrived/reached' and is the clause's verb; هنگام روز is an adverbial phrase 'at the time of day / at daybreak' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s303-g118: چو 'when' introduces the temporal image; بر زد سر is a verbal phrase where زد (literally 'struck') with object سر (head) idiomatically means 'lifted/raised its head' (i.e. the sun rose); از چرخ گیتی فروز is a prepositional phrase 'from the (wheel of the) bright world/sky', with فروز postpositive as an epithet 'bright/illuminating', producing the poetic image of the world/sun lifting its bright head from the sky.
segments.s303-g119: تهمتن is the subject (an epithet for the hero); کمان را uses the postposition را to mark the definite direct object 'the bow'; به زه is a prepositional phrase 'to/against the bow‑string' (به + زه); برنهاد is the past, 3rd‑person singular verb 'he placed/laid upon', so the clause means 'Tahmten set the bow to the string' (i.e. strung or prepared the bow).
segments.s303-g120: چو نزدیک شد is a temporal clause 'when he came near' (چو = when; نزدیک = near/approaching; شد = became/came); ترگ بر سر نهاد is the main action: ترگ 'a rag/cloth' as object, بر سر 'on (the) head', and نهاد past 3rd‑person singular 'he placed', so 'he put a rag on his head'. The verb comes at the end as in standard Persian word order.
segments.s303-g33: چو here introduces a simile (‘like’). The two proper names گرگین, میلاد (Gorgin, Milad) and رهام (Roham) are given before the predicate noun شیر (‘lion’), with the copula omitted as is normal in Persian poetry: the line reads ‘as Gorgin, Milad and Roham (were) lions.’ Word order is Name(s) + predicate noun.
segments.s303-g34: هجیر is a proper name and is the subject; و simply joins phrases. چو again marks likeness (‘as/like’) before شیدوش (here a name in context). گرد is a poetic/archaic verbal element (from گردیدن) used like ‘became’ or ‘turned’ and is followed by the predicate adjective دلیر (‘brave’). In short: ‘Hejir, and as Shidush, became brave.’
segments.s303-g35: Two personal names (فریبرز, کاووس) are placed before a locative prepositional phrase بر میمنه (‘on the right side/flank’). The verb ‘to be’ is omitted; the phrase describes their position: ‘Fereybarz and Kavus (stood) on the right flank.’
segments.s303-g36: سپاهی (‘the army/soldiers’) is qualified by همه (‘all’) and two coordinated adjectival phrases یک‌دل و یک‌تنه (‘one‑hearted and one‑bodied/together’). There is no explicit copula; this is a predicative nominal construction typical of classical Persian poetry: ‘the army was all united in heart and body.’
segments.s303-g37: منوچهر is the proper subject (the king's name). بر is the preposition introducing the locus/position (literally 'on/at'). میسره here functions as the noun/adjective describing that locus (an attainable/secure place). جای داشت is a light verb construction: جای (place) + داشت (past 'had') meaning 'occupied/held a place.' Word order is Subject — prepositional phrase — place + past verb.
segments.s303-g38: که is the relative particle 'who/that' linking this clause to the previous. با + جنگ ('with battle') marks accompaniment or context ('in battle'); هر جنگیی is an emphatic/quantifying phrase 'every kind/each battle'; پای داشت is a compound where پای ('foot/footing') + داشت ('had') means 'had a footing' i.e. 'stood firm.' The verb داشت is past, so the whole clause describes his habitual/past stance in all battles.
segments.s303-g39: به پشت سپه is a locative phrase 'at/to the back of the army' with به marking location. The two proper names گیو گودرز follow as the nominal subject. بود is the past copula 'was/were,' so the clause literally means 'at the army's rear were Giv and Goudarz.' Word order is locative phrase — names — copula (poetic inversion).
segments.s303-g40: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a descriptive clause. پشت here means 'support/backing' rather than simply 'back,' and و نگهبان links it with نگهبان 'guardian/guard.' هر مرز ('every border/frontier') is the object of their guarding. بود is the past copula 'was/were,' so the clause says they served as the support and guardians of every frontier.
